tags: 強い vs 弱い pair
lesson:
酒さけに弱よわい — weak against alcohol, a lightweight. This pattern — Xに弱い — is one of the most useful things about this word. It means you're susceptible to X, you can't handle it. 暑あつさに弱よわい (bad with heat). プレッシャーに弱よわい (cracks under pressure). The に marks what defeats you.

After a number, 弱 means "slightly less than": 三十分弱さんじゅっぷんじゃく (a little under thirty minutes). Its opposite 強きょう means "slightly more than": 百人強ひゃくにんきょう (just over 100 people). This is a genuinely useful trick that no dictionary definition of "weak" would prepare you for.

The kanji 弱 shows two bows (弓ゆみ) with decorative marks — a bow that's ornamental rather than functional. It looks like a weapon but can't shoot. The appearance of strength without the substance.

Dictionaryよわい
Negativeよわくない
Pastよわかった
Past negよわくなかった
Adverbよわく
て-formよわくて

耳みみが痛いたい doesn't always mean your ear hurts. It can mean something you heard was painfully true — criticism that hit home. 耳みみにする (to happen to hear something). 耳みみが早はやい (to have fast ears — to hear news quickly, to be well-informed). The ear in Japanese is the organ of being alert, being connected to the world.

The kanji 耳 is one of the most faithful pictographs surviving into modern writing. The oracle bone version was a drawing of an ear, and the modern form still looks like one — the outer rectangle is the ear shape, the inner horizontal lines are the curves of the canal. It's also a radical in other kanji: 聞ぶん (to hear/ask — an ear at a gate), 聴ちょう (to listen carefully).

Sound anchor: mi-mi — the doubled sound is itself worth noting. Japanese has several body-part words with repeated syllables: 耳 (みみ, ear), 目め (eye — not doubled, but paired with 耳 constantly). The two senses appear together in phrases like 目めと耳みみを使つかう (use your eyes and ears — pay close attention).

Before chairs arrived from the West, Japan sat on the floor. 座る originally meant exactly that — settling onto the ground or a cushion, folding your legs underneath you. The kanji 座 shows people (人ひと人ひと) on earth (土つち) under a roof (广). People sitting on the ground inside a building. That's what sitting looked like for most of Japanese history.

Modern 座る works for chairs too: 椅子いすに座すわる (sit in a chair). ここに座すわってください (please sit here). But the floor-sitting origin persists in vocabulary: 正座せいざ (formal kneeling — "correct sitting"), the legs-folded-underneath position that numbs your feet after ten minutes. 座布団ざぶとん (floor cushion) — the cushion that makes floor-sitting bearable.

Particles: 座る takes に — the location where you sit. 椅子いすに座すわる. 床ゆかに座すわる (sit on the floor).

Passive に座られる: に marks the sitter. 隣となりに座すわられた (someone sat next to me — and I didn't want them to). Adversative passive — their sitting affected you.

Causative を／に座らせる: を leans coercive. 子供こどもを椅子いすに座すわらせる (make the kid sit in the chair).

The kanji 右 is a hand (ナ) plus a mouth (口くち). A hand reaching toward the mouth — and since most people eat with their right hand, that hand became "right." Its twin 左ひだり (left) has a hand plus a tool-like shape — the hand that holds instruments. Right eats, left works. The ancient division of labor is frozen in the characters.

右みぎに曲まがる (turn right). 右手みぎて (right hand). 右側みぎがわ (right side). In Japan, traffic drives on the left, pedestrians keep left on escalators in Osaka (but right in Tokyo — a regional quirk that confuses even Japanese people). Direction words get daily use.

右 in compounds read as う carries political or metaphorical weight: 右翼うよく (right wing — political). 左右さゆう (left and right — and also "influence," as in 結果けっかを左右さゆうする, to influence an outcome, to sway it left and right).

浴びる doesn't mean "to take a bath" — that's おふろに入る. 浴びる means to have something pour over you, to be drenched in it. The most common use: シャワーを浴あびる (take a shower). But it extends beyond water: 日光にっこうを浴あびる (bathe in sunlight). 注目ちゅうもくを浴あびる (bathe in attention — be in the spotlight). 批判ひはんを浴あびる (be showered with criticism).

The pattern is always the same: something pours down on you and you receive it with your whole body. Water, light, praise, blame — the image is of standing under a cascade. The kanji 浴よく has 氵 (water) and 谷たに (valley) — water flowing down from a valley onto you.

Particles: 浴びる takes を — the thing pouring over you is the direct object. シャワーを浴あびる. 批判ひはんを浴あびる.

Potential が浴びられる: が. ここで朝日あさひが浴あびられる (you can catch the morning sun here).

Passive に浴びせられる: more commonly, the transitive 浴あびせる (to pour onto someone) is used in passive. 水みずを浴あびせられた (water was poured on me).

Causative に…を浴びさせる: に marks who you drench, を marks what. 子供こどもにシャワーを浴あびさせる (have the kid take a shower).

肩かたがこる — stiff shoulders. This might be the most culturally loaded body complaint in Japan. Where Americans get back pain and Germans get circulation problems, Japanese people get 肩かたこり (shoulder stiffness). It's discussed so universally that some linguists have argued the Japanese experience shoulder tension more because they have a word for it — the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis in action, or just a nation that spends a lot of time hunched over desks.

肩かたの荷にが下おりる (the load on my shoulders came down) — a burden was lifted. 肩かたを持もつ (hold someone's shoulder) — to take someone's side. 肩身かたみが狭せまい (narrow-shouldered feeling) — to feel ashamed or out of place. Shoulders in Japanese carry responsibility, allegiance, and social comfort.

The kanji 肩 has 戸と (door) on top and the flesh radical 月 below. The shoulder as the body's door-frame — the flat platform where arms hinge. Architecturally apt.

もう寝ねる — I'm going to bed. Three syllables, conversation over, goodnight. 寝る covers both going to sleep and lying down. 昨日きのうは早はやく寝ねた (went to bed early). ソファで寝ねていた (was lying on the sofa — maybe asleep, maybe just horizontal).

寝る vs 眠る: 寝る is the physical act — getting into bed, lying down. 眠ねむる is the state of being asleep, the unconsciousness itself. You can 寝る without 眠る (lie in bed unable to sleep). You can 眠る while sitting up on a train. 寝ねても眠ねむれない (I'm in bed but I can't sleep) uses both to make the distinction explicit.

One more: 寝坊ねぼうする (to oversleep) — a compound you'll need. 寝ね (sleep) + 坊ぼう (a suffix that implies someone behaving like a child). Oversleeping as childish indulgence.

Particles: 寝る is intransitive. Use で for where: ベッドで寝ねる. No direct object.

Passive に寝られる: adversative — someone's sleeping affects you. 赤あかちゃんに横よこで寝ねられて動うごけない (the baby fell asleep next to me and I can't move).

Causative を／に寝させる: 子供こどもを早はやく寝ねさせる (put the kid to bed early).

消す makes things disappear. 電気でんきを消けす (turn off the light). テレビを消けす (turn off the TV). 火ひを消けす (put out a fire). 文字もじを消けす (erase text). データを消けす (delete data). The common thread: something that was present — light, sound, flame, information — stops existing. You remove it from the world.

Its intransitive partner 消きえる is when things vanish on their own: 電気でんきが消きえた (the light went out). 雪ゆきが消きえた (the snow melted away). You 消す — you cause the disappearance. Something 消えるs — it fades by itself.

Particles: 消す takes を — the thing you extinguish is the direct object. 電気でんきを消けす.

Potential が消せる: が. このデータが消けせない (I can't delete this data).

Passive に消される: に. 大事だいじなファイルを消けされた (my important file was deleted).

Causative に…を消させる: に marks who you make erase, を marks what. 生徒せいとに黒板こくばんを消けさせる (have the student erase the blackboard).

元気げんきでね — take care. Said when someone is leaving for a long time, carrying a warmth that "goodbye" can't match. It literally means "be well" — stay in your original energy, keep being alive in that buzzing way. This single phrase captures what 元気 really means: not just "fine" but vitally, actively well.

元げん (origin) + 気き (energy/spirit) = original energy. The baseline life force. 元気げんきな子供こども is a kid who won't sit still. 元気げんきがない is the opposite — something's wrong, the spark is out. 元気げんきを出だす (muster energy — pull yourself together, cheer up).

The 気 half of this word is one of the most connected kanji in Japanese. 天気てんき (weather — the sky's energy). 気持きもち (feelings — where energy is held). 人気にんき (popularity — people's energy gathered around something). 気分きぶん (mood — the portion of energy you're feeling right now). These aren't random compounds. They're all variations on a single idea: qi, the invisible force that moves through everything. 元気 is that force at its source.

Dictionaryげんき
Negativeげんきじゃない
Pastげんきだった
Past negげんきじゃなかった
Adverbげんきに
て-formげんきで

全部ぜんぶ食たべた (ate everything). 全部ぜんぶでいくら？ (how much for everything?). 全部 is the blunt, no-remainder "all" — every single part, nothing left out. It works both as a noun and as an adverb, and it's the word you reach for when you want to emphasize totality.

The kanji: 全ぜん (all, complete) + 部ぶ (part, section). All parts. The word is reassuringly literal. 全 shows up in other totality words: 全然ぜんぜん (not at all / completely), 全員ぜんいん (everyone — all members), 全国ぜんこく (nationwide).

全部 vs 全て vs みんな: All mean "everything/all," different flavors. 全部 is the most concrete — every piece, every item. 全すべて is more literary and abstract — "all things." みんな usually means "everyone" (people) but can mean "everything" casually. For physical objects and countable things, 全部 is the default.

去きょ means "to leave, to depart." 年ねん means "year." The year that left — last year. Clean, visual, immediate. 去年きょねんの夏なつ (last summer). 去年きょねんはどうでしたか (how was last year?).

Note the prefix pattern breaks here. For weeks and months, you use 先せん for "last" (先週せんしゅう, 先月せんげつ). For years, it's 去 instead. There's no 先年 in common use. This is one of those places where the system isn't perfectly regular — you just have to know that years are different.

The 去 kanji appears in 過去かこ (the past) — literally "passed and departed." Once something is 去, it's gone. Not coming back.

The kanji 引ひき is a bow (弓ゆみ) with a vertical line — the string pulled taut. Drawing a bowstring is the original act of pulling, and the kanji preserves it perfectly.

引く does more work than English "pull." ドアを引ひく (pull a door). 辞書じしょを引ひく (look up in a dictionary — "pull out" information). 風邪かぜを引ひく (catch a cold — you "draw" the illness to yourself). 線せんを引ひく (draw a line). 引ひく手てあまた (more offers than you can handle — everyone pulling at you). The thread across all uses: bringing something toward you, or drawing something out.

The opposite is 押おす (to push). You'll see 引ひ and 押お on doors everywhere — 引く to pull open, 押す to push open. And 引ひっ越こす (to move house) literally means to pull up and cross over — uprooting yourself.

Particles: 引く takes を — the thing you pull is the direct object. ドアを引ひく. 辞書じしょを引ひく.

Potential が引ける: が. この戸とが引ひけない (I can't pull this door open).

Passive に引かれる: に marks who pulls. 彼かれの才能さいのうに引ひかれた (I was drawn to his talent).

Causative に…を引かせる: に marks who pulls, を marks what. 馬うまに荷車にぐるまを引ひかせる (have the horse pull the cart).

The 館かん suffix is your "building" marker: 映画館えいがかん (movie theater), 美術館びじゅつかん (art museum), 旅館りょかん (Japanese inn). Once you spot 館, you know you're talking about a building with a purpose. 図書館 is 図書としょ (books as a collection) + 館かん (hall). A hall of books.

図書 isn't the word for a single book (that's 本ほん). It implies a collection, an archive — institutional books. 図書としょカード (library card). 図書室としょしつ is a smaller version — a library room in a school, using 室しつ (room) instead of 館 (hall).

図書館としょかんで勉強べんきょうする (study at the library) is a staple of student life. 図書館としょかんの本ほんを借かりる (borrow a library book) — note 借かりる (to borrow), not 貸かす (to lend). You borrow; the library lends.

上げる has two major lives. First: to physically raise something. 手てを上あげる (raise your hand). Its intransitive partner 上あがる is when something rises on its own: 温度おんどが上あがる (the temperature rises). You 上げる things. Things 上がる by themselves.

Second life: to give (to someone of equal or lower status). プレゼントをあげる (give a present). And in the 〜てあげる pattern, it means doing something as a favor: 教おしえてあげる (I'll teach you — as a kindness). This giving sense is so common many learners meet it before the "raise" meaning.

The two aren't disconnected. Giving is an upward motion — you lift something from your side and hand it over. In classical Japanese, offering to a superior meant literally raising it up to them. The physical gesture and the social act share a direction.

Particles: For raising: を. 手てを上あげる. For giving: に marks the recipient, を marks the gift. 友達ともだちにお菓子かしをあげる.

Potential が上げられる: が. 給料きゅうりょうが上あげられない (can't raise the salary).

Passive に上げられる: に. 税金ぜいきんを上あげられた (taxes were raised on us).

Causative に…を上げさせる: に marks who raises, を marks what. 生徒せいとに手てを上あげさせる (have students raise their hands).

Traffic lights in Japan are called 青信号あおしんごう — "blue" signal. They're green. 青野菜あおやさい — "blue" vegetables. They're green. 青々あおあおとした草くさ — lush "blue" grass. Definitely green. What's happening: historically, Japanese 青 covered the entire blue-to-green spectrum. 緑 existed as a separate word, but green was considered a shade of blue, not its own category.

The boundary has been shifting toward the English distinction over the past century, but the old system lingers everywhere. And 緑 is a noun, not an adjective — you can't say 緑い. To modify a noun, use 緑の: 緑みどりの葉は (green leaves). This puts it in a different grammatical class from 赤あかい, 青あおい, 白しろい, 黒くろい — all い-adjectives. Green didn't earn its own adjective form because it wasn't considered a basic color.

The kanji has the silk radical 糸いと — green was a key dye color for silk and fabric. 緑茶りょくちゃ (green tea) uses the on'yomi りょく. 新緑しんりょく (the fresh green of new spring leaves) is a seasonal word that evokes a specific time of year.

腕うでがいい doesn't mean "nice arms." It means "skilled." 腕うでを磨みがく (polish your arms) means to hone your skills. 腕うでの見みせどころ (a place to show your arms) means a chance to show what you can do. 腕前うでまえ means ability level. Your arms are your tools, and what you do with them defines your capability. The figurative meaning is at least as common as the physical one.

For the literal body part: うで means the arm from shoulder to wrist. 腕時計うでどけい (wristwatch — arm-clock). 腕組うでぐみ (folding your arms) — a gesture of thinking, skepticism, or defiance, much as in Western body language.

Three words for "door," each for a different kind. ドア: Western-style, hinged, swings open — houses, cars, building entrances. 戸と: traditional Japanese, slides — the default in older-style rooms. 扉とびら: large, formal — double doors, grand entrances, or metaphorical doors ("the door to a new era"). Your bedroom probably has a ドア. The tatami room has a 戸 or ふすま.

ドアを開あける (open the door). ドアを閉しめる (close the door). ドアをノックする (knock on the door) — ノック is another English loan. Western doors came with Western verbs for using them.

女おんな (woman) + の + 子こ (child) = a female child. Transparent construction. But the age range stretches: a group of women in their twenties might casually call themselves 女の子たち. It carries warmth and youthfulness that 女性じょせい (woman — formal) and 女おんな (woman — blunt) don't.

Choosing wrong has consequences: 女の子 (warm, youthful) → 女おんなの人ひと (neutral polite) → 女性じょせい (formal, respectful) → 女おんな (direct, can sound rude). When in doubt, 女性 is safe. Calling a grown woman 女の子 can be affectionate or patronizing depending on context. Calling her 女 flatly is almost always too blunt.

The kanji 男おとこ is 田た (rice field) over 力ちから (power/strength). Power in the rice field. For thousands of years, this was the definition of maleness across East Asia — not warrior strength but agricultural labor, the muscle that feeds the family. The character hasn't changed, even as the job description has.

男の子 works the same way as 女の子: 男おとこ + の + 子こ = a male child, a boy. Like its counterpart, the age range is flexible. かわいい男おとこの子こ could describe a toddler or a twenty-year-old, depending on who's talking. The word is inherently warm and light — never formal, never harsh.

Register matters: 男の子 (casual, affectionate) → 男おとこの人ひと (neutral polite) → 男性だんせい (formal) → 男おとこ (blunt, rough). The formal 男性 uses the Chinese-derived reading だん; the casual words use native おとこ. In Japanese, Chinese readings almost always signal higher formality. Keep that principle and you can predict register across hundreds of words.

Japanese makes plurals by adding suffixes. 私わたし (I) + たち = we. Same たち works on anything: 子供こどもたち (children), 彼かれたち (they). The word 友達ともだち (friend) already has 達たち — the kanji form of たち — baked in.

たち isn't the only option. ら is rougher: 僕ぼくら (we — masculine casual), 俺おれら (we — masculine rough). たち is softer, polite, works everywhere.

And just like 私 alone, 私たち gets dropped when the group reference is obvious. Japanese prefers implicit subjects. 明日あした行いく over 私わたしたちは明日あした行いく when it's clear you mean the group. Using 私たち explicitly adds emphasis — "we are the ones going" — or claims collective identity: 私わたしたちの学校がっこう (our school).

Duplicate Also appears as word #222.

この近ちかくにコンビニある？ — you'll say this sentence in Japan before you say almost anything else. 近ちかく is what you get when you take 近ちかい (word 74) and shift it from adjective to standalone word. It works as both a noun — the vicinity, the nearby area — and an adverb — soon, before long.

駅えきの近ちかく (near the station — a location, not a description). 近ちかくの店みせ (a nearby shop). 近ちかくに住すんでいる (I live nearby). It takes に and の like any other location word — 上うえ, 中なか, 下した all work the same way.

The time meaning catches people off guard: 近ちかく届とどくと思おもいます (I think it'll arrive before long). Close in space, close in time — same word handles both.

meaning: to do, perform; to give (to inferiors/animals)
lesson:
Duplicate Also appears as word #413.

する and やる both mean "to do," but they don't feel the same. する is neutral and polished — it shows up in formal writing, paired with する-nouns. やる is casual and physical. It implies you're actually getting your hands in it: やるしかない (nothing to do but do it). やってみる (give it a try). やった！ (I did it!).

In casual conversation, やる replaces する almost everywhere: いつやる？ (when are we doing this?). もうやった (already did it). 宿題しゅくだいやった？ (did you do your homework?). In formal or written settings, する stays dominant.

There's a second meaning: "to give" — but only downward. To plants, animals, or people lower in status. 花はなに水みずをやる (water the flowers). 犬いぬにえさをやる (feed the dog). For giving to people, use あげる.

Particles を for the thing you do: 仕事しごとをやる. For "give," add に for the recipient: 猫ねこにえさをやる.

テスト、かなり難むずかしかった (that test was pretty damn hard). Not the hardest test of your life, but harder than you expected. That's where かなり lives — "considerably," "quite," the word for when something exceeds your expectations without blowing you away.

Among Japanese intensity adverbs, it sits in the upper-middle range. ちょっと (a bit) < けっこう (fairly, more than figured) < かなり (quite, considerably) < とても (very). The gap between けっこう and かなり: けっこう is mildly surprised, かなり is genuinely impressed. けっこう大おおきい (oh, it's fairly big) vs. かなり大おおきい (that's really quite big).

かなり疲つかれた (I'm really tired). 日本語にほんごがかなり上手じょうずですね (your Japanese is quite good). Before nouns, it needs の: かなりの人ひとが来きた (quite a lot of people came). Before adjectives and verbs, it stands alone.

The oldest ancestor of this character was 或 — a weapon (戈ほこ) defending a patch of land, a guarded territory. In bronze script, an outer wall 囗 was added around it, making the borders explicit: 國. Japan's postwar script reform simplified the interior to 玉たま, giving us the modern 国くに. That simplification was practical shorthand — though people like to read "the treasure within the borders" into it, that wasn't the intent.

Two readings, split along the usual pattern. くに is native Japanese, used when the word stands alone or carries personal weight: 国くにに帰かえる (go back to my country — home). こく is Sino-Japanese, used in compounds: 外国がいこく (foreign country), 国語こくご (national language — what Japanese is called as a school subject), 国際こくさい (international).

お国くにはどちら？ can mean "what country are you from?" but it can also mean "which part of Japan?" Before the modern nation-state concept arrived, 国くに referred to provinces and feudal domains. That older sense lingers in polite conversation and regional dialect.

The kanji 起き puts together 走そう (run) and 己こ (self) — a body rising into motion. From that physical sense of getting up, it extends to events rising into existence. 起おこる is specifically about things happening: accidents, problems, phenomena.

事故じこが起おこった (an accident happened). 問題もんだいが起おこる (a problem arises). 戦争せんそうが起おこる (a war breaks out). The event is always the grammatical subject, marked with が. You don't make things 起おこる — they 起おこる on their own.

When something was caused deliberately, reach for the transitive partner 起おこす: 事故じこを起おこす (cause an accident). 行動こうどうを起おこす (take action). 起おこす also means to wake someone: 子供こどもを起おこす (wake the kid up).

Don't mix up 起おこる with 起おきる. Both can mean "to happen" — 事件じけんが起おきた and 事件じけんが起おこった are interchangeable. But 起おきる also means "to wake up" or "to get up" — its most common meaning in daily conversation. 起おこる never means "to wake up."

Particles が marks the event. The particle doesn't shift — 起おこる is inherently about things arising on their own. For causing something, switch to 起おこす (を).
